name: Sharp-shinned Hawk
ecosystem: Forest (breeding). Coniferous and mixed forest. Wide habitat range in migration and winter. Appears in suburban areas as winter visitor.
season: Resident and migratory (year-round PNW presence; substantial fall/spring migration counts)
conservation: Least Concern (IUCN). DDT-recovered. Long-term hawkwatch count data (Hawk Mountain since 1930s) documents decline and recovery. Trichomonosis contemporary concern.

vocalization.description: High, thin, rapid series: kik-kik-kik (also ki-ki-ki). Highest frequency of the three accipiter alarms in the engine.
vocalization.function: Nest defense, alarm. Generally quiet outside breeding season; migration birds largely silent.
vocalization.notable: Highest-frequency accipiter call. Easily missed in forest acoustic clutter. Requires listener attention. Opposite of the Goshawk's unmissable alarm.

size.dimorphism: Most extreme sexual dimorphism of the three PNW accipiters. Male barely larger than a robin.
size.id_challenge: Overlaps with Cooper's Hawk in size. Square/notched tail tip (Sharp-shin) vs. rounded (Cooper's) is primary mark.

migration.abundance: Most numerically abundant accipiter at migration count sites. More migratory than Cooper's or Goshawk.
migration.citizen_science: Cornerstone species for hawkwatch citizen science programs. Hawk Mountain Sanctuary (PA) continuous data since 1930s.

connections.description: Hovercraft (E=5). No-vocals duo. Both defined by precision within constraint. Both easy to overlook.
Northern Goshawk. Largest. The trio: Goshawk(225) -> Cooper's(226) -> Sharp-shin(227).
Cooper's Hawk. The ID problem pair. Square vs. rounded tail. Classic birder challenge.
Red-tailed Hawk. FIRST BUTEO. Accipiter-to-buteo transition. Forest interior to open country.
